A focused assessment is performed when a client presents with a specific problem and involves collecting data related to that particular body system. In this case, the client presents with respiratory symptoms such as chest congestion and cough, indicating a need for targeted assessment. This approach allows the nurse to efficiently gather relevant information, including lung sounds, respiratory rate, sputum characteristics, and oxygenation status. Focused assessments are essential for identifying current issues without performing unnecessary comprehensive evaluations.

(Correct Option 3). Data related to the respiratory system should be collected because the client is presenting with respiratory symptoms. A focused assessment ensures relevant and efficient evaluation of the problem.

(Option 1). Follow-up care data is appropriate when monitoring a previously diagnosed condition, but this situation involves new or worsening symptoms requiring targeted assessment.

(Option 2). A complete health database is unnecessary because the client’s concern is specific and does not require a full-body assessment.

(Option 4). Data related only to prior treatment for the cold is insufficient, as the nurse must assess the current respiratory symptoms to determine the client’s condition.

Educational Objective
A focused assessment is used when a client presents with specific symptoms. It involves collecting data related to the affected body system to efficiently identify and manage the problem.
questionList.subject: Health Assessment
questionList.title: Focused Assessment

answerChoiceList.choice: Data related to the respiratory system
Data related to the treatment for the cold

questionList.questionText: A nurse is preparing to auscultate for the presence of bowel sounds in a client who has just undergone surgery. The nurse places the stethoscope in which abdominal quadrant first?
questionList.explanationText:
Auscultation of bowel sounds is an essential part of abdominal assessment, especially in postoperative clients to evaluate gastrointestinal motility. The nurse should begin in the right lower quadrant (RLQ) because this area contains the ileocecal valve, where bowel sounds are typically most active and easiest to detect. Bowel sounds are produced by peristalsis as contents move through the intestines. Starting in the RLQ increases the likelihood of hearing sounds quickly, especially when bowel activity may be reduced after surgery. Proper technique involves using the diaphragm of the stethoscope and listening systematically across all quadrants to assess frequency and characteristics of bowel sounds.

(Correct Option 4). The nurse begins auscultation in the right lower quadrant because it is closest to the ileocecal valve, where bowel sounds are usually most active and detectable.

(Option 1). The left upper quadrant is not the preferred starting point because bowel sounds may be less prominent in this area initially.

(Option 2). The left lower quadrant is also not the optimal starting location, as bowel sounds are typically first heard more clearly in the RLQ.

(Option 3). The right upper quadrant contains structures such as the liver, and bowel sounds are not typically assessed first in this area.

Educational Objective
Bowel sound auscultation should begin in the right lower quadrant near the ileocecal valve, where sounds are most active, ensuring efficient and accurate abdominal assessment.
questionList.subject: Health Assessment
questionList.title: Abdominal Assessment

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Basic Care and Comfort

questionList.questionText: A nurse performing a genital examination of a male client notes that the skin of the penis and scrotum is wrinkled. On the basis of this finding, the nurse:
questionList.explanationText:
The skin of the penis and scrotum normally appears loose and wrinkled due to the presence of the dartos muscle and the need for temperature regulation of the testes. This wrinkling allows the scrotum to contract or relax in response to temperature changes, helping maintain optimal conditions for sperm production. In a normal adult male, the scrotal skin is thin, pigmented, and rugated (wrinkled). This finding is a normal anatomical and physiological characteristic and does not indicate pathology such as infection or mass. Understanding normal variations is essential to avoid unnecessary interventions and to distinguish between normal and abnormal findings during assessment.

(Correct Option 1). Wrinkled skin of the penis and scrotum is a normal finding due to the presence of the dartos muscle and temperature regulation mechanisms, so the nurse should document it as normal.

(Option 2). Wrinkling does not indicate infection. Penile discharge would be assessed separately if infection is suspected based on other symptoms.

(Option 3). Wrinkling is not associated with the presence of a mass. Masses would be detected through palpation, not by observing normal skin texture.

(Option 4). Additional subjective data is unnecessary because this is a normal anatomical finding and does not indicate an abnormality.

Educational Objective
Wrinkled (rugated) skin of the penis and scrotum is a normal finding related to temperature regulation. Nurses should recognize normal anatomy to avoid unnecessary concern or intervention.
questionList.subject: Health Assessment
questionList.title: Male Genital Assessment

answerChoiceList.choice: Checks for penile discharge, because this finding indicates infection
Palpates for a mass in the scrotum, because wrinkling indicates the presence of one
Obtains additional subjective data from the client, focusing on the scrotal abnormality

questionList.questionText: A nurse is preparing to check the breath sounds of a client. Over which anatomic area does the nurse place the stethoscope when auscultating for bronchial breath sounds?
questionList.explanationText:
Breath sounds are classified into vesicular, bronchovesicular, and bronchial sounds based on their location and characteristics. Bronchial breath sounds are high-pitched, loud, and have a hollow quality, typically heard over the trachea and larynx. These sounds are produced by air moving through the large airways. In contrast, vesicular sounds are soft and low-pitched and are heard over the peripheral lung fields, while bronchovesicular sounds are intermediate and heard near the mainstem bronchi. Accurate placement of the stethoscope is critical to distinguish between these sounds and to identify abnormal findings such as consolidation or airway obstruction. Recognizing the normal location of bronchial sounds helps prevent misinterpretation during respiratory assessment.

(Correct Option 1). Bronchial breath sounds are best heard over the trachea or larynx because this is where large airway airflow produces loud, high-pitched sounds. Proper placement in this area ensures accurate identification of bronchial sounds.

(Option 2). Auscultating over peripheral lung fields assesses vesicular breath sounds, not bronchial sounds, as airflow is more dispersed in smaller airways.

(Option 3). The area between the scapulae is where bronchovesicular sounds are typically heard, not pure bronchial sounds.

(Option 4). Lower lung lobes are associated with vesicular sounds, which are softer and not characteristic of bronchial breath sounds.

Educational Objective
Bronchial breath sounds are normally heard over the trachea and larynx. Correct identification of breath sound locations helps distinguish normal from abnormal respiratory findings.
questionList.subject: Health Assessment
questionList.title: Respiratory Assessment

answerChoiceList.choice: Over the lower lobes of the lungs

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Basic Care and Comfort

questionList.questionText: The nurse, performing an abdominal examination, inspects the client's abdomen. Which assessment technique does the nurse perform next?
questionList.explanationText:
The abdominal assessment follows a specific sequence: inspection, auscultation, percussion, and palpation. This order is important because performing palpation or percussion before auscultation can alter bowel sounds. Bowel sounds are produced by intestinal movement, and any mechanical stimulation such as pressing or tapping can increase or decrease their activity, leading to inaccurate findings. Therefore, after inspection, the nurse should immediately proceed to auscultation to assess the frequency, pitch, and character of bowel sounds without interference. This sequence ensures accurate evaluation of gastrointestinal motility, detection of abnormalities like ileus or bowel obstruction, and supports proper clinical decision-making.

(Correct Option 2). Auscultation is performed immediately after inspection because it allows the nurse to listen to bowel sounds without disturbing intestinal activity. Performing auscultation first ensures an accurate baseline assessment of gastrointestinal function and prevents false findings caused by manipulation of the abdomen.

(Option 1). Percussion is performed after auscultation, not immediately after inspection. If done earlier, it can stimulate bowel activity and alter the natural sounds, making auscultation findings unreliable.

(Option 3). Light palpation is done later in the assessment. Performing it too early may increase peristalsis and interfere with the accuracy of bowel sound assessment during auscultation.

(Option 4). Deep palpation is the last step of abdominal assessment and is used to evaluate underlying structures and masses. It should not be performed before auscultation because it significantly alters bowel activity.

Educational Objective
The correct sequence of abdominal examination is inspection, auscultation, percussion, and palpation. Auscultation must always follow inspection to avoid altering bowel sounds and to ensure accurate gastrointestinal assessment.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Abdominal Assessment

questionList.questionText: A community health nurse is instructing a group of female clients about breast self-examination (BSE). The nurse instructs the clients to perform the examination:
questionList.explanationText:
Breast self-examination (BSE) is an important screening tool used for early detection of breast abnormalities, including lumps and changes suggestive of breast cancer. The timing of BSE is crucial because hormonal fluctuations during the menstrual cycle affect breast tissue. During menstruation, increased levels of hormones such as estrogen and progesterone can cause breast swelling, tenderness, and nodularity. Therefore, the most accurate time to perform BSE is when hormonal influence is minimal and breast tissue is least congested. This occurs about one week after menstruation begins, when the breasts are softer and less tender, allowing for better detection of abnormal findings. Consistency in timing each month also helps individuals recognize normal versus abnormal changes.

(Correct Option 4). Performing BSE one week after menstruation begins ensures that hormonal effects on breast tissue are minimal. At this time, the breasts are less swollen and tender, making it easier to detect lumps or abnormalities accurately and consistently each month.

(Option 1). At the onset of menstruation, the breasts are often swollen and tender due to hormonal changes. This can make examination uncomfortable and may lead to inaccurate findings.

(Option 2). During ovulation, hormonal fluctuations still occur, which can affect breast tissue consistency. This is not the optimal time for accurate breast examination.

(Option 3). Weekly examinations are unnecessary and may lead to anxiety or confusion due to normal cyclic changes. Monthly examination at the correct time is recommended instead.

Educational Objective
Breast self-examination should be performed monthly, ideally one week after menstruation begins, when breast tissue is least influenced by hormones, allowing for accurate detection of abnormalities.
questionList.subject: Community Health Nursing
questionList.title: Breast Self-Examination

answerChoiceList.choice: Weekly, at the same time of day

questionList.questionText: A nurse is assessing a client for the major risk factors associated with coronary artery disease (CAD). Which modifiable risk factor does the nurse obtain data on from the client?
questionList.explanationText:
Coronary artery disease (CAD) develops when the coronary arteries become narrowed or blocked due to atherosclerosis, a process involving lipid deposition, inflammation, and plaque formation within the arterial wall. Several risk factors contribute to CAD, and these are classified as modifiable or nonmodifiable. Modifiable risk factors are those that can be treated, controlled, or changed through lifestyle changes or medical management, such as hypertension, smoking, obesity, diabetes, and hyperlipidemia. In contrast, age, ethnicity, and genetic inheritance are nonmodifiable because they cannot be altered. Identifying modifiable risks is essential because targeted interventions can reduce cardiovascular morbidity and mortality.

(Correct Option 3). Hypertension is a major modifiable risk factor for coronary artery disease because elevated blood pressure damages arterial walls, promotes atherosclerotic plaque formation, and increases cardiac workload. The nurse should obtain data about hypertension because it can be controlled through medications, diet, exercise, and other therapeutic measures, making it a key focus of prevention and management.

(Option 1). Age is an important risk factor for CAD, but it is nonmodifiable. Although increasing age raises the likelihood of atherosclerotic changes and cardiovascular disease, it cannot be changed through treatment or lifestyle intervention.

(Option 2). Ethnicity may influence CAD risk because some populations have a higher prevalence of hypertension, diabetes, or heart disease. However, ethnicity is a nonmodifiable characteristic and is not considered a changeable risk factor.

(Option 4). Genetic inheritance refers to family history or hereditary predisposition to CAD. This is also a nonmodifiable risk factor because inherited traits cannot be altered, though awareness of family history helps guide screening and prevention.

Educational Objective
Risk factors for coronary artery disease are categorized as modifiable and nonmodifiable. Hypertension is a modifiable risk factor that can be treated and controlled, while age, ethnicity, and genetic inheritance are nonmodifiable factors.
questionList.subject: Adult Health Nursing
questionList.title: Coronary Artery Disease Risk Factors

questionList.questionText: A client complains that he feels as though his ear is blocked and tells the nurse that he has a history of cerumen impaction in the external ear. The nurse, inspecting the ears for cerumen impaction, checks for:
questionList.explanationText:
Cerumen, commonly called earwax, is a normal protective secretion produced in the external auditory canal. It helps trap dust, microorganisms, and other foreign particles while also lubricating the canal. However, excessive accumulation can lead to cerumen impaction, which may cause a sensation of ear fullness, decreased hearing, discomfort, tinnitus, or a blocked ear. On inspection, cerumen impaction is identified by the presence of yellowish, tan, or brownish waxy material obstructing the canal. This finding differs from signs of infection or inflammation, which usually include redness, swelling, or edema. Recognizing the characteristic appearance of cerumen is essential for accurate nursing assessment and safe ear care.

(Correct Option 4). A yellowish or brownish waxy material in the external auditory canal is the classic finding associated with cerumen impaction. This material may partially or completely block the canal and explains the client’s complaint of a blocked ear sensation. Identifying this appearance helps confirm the presence of impacted cerumen rather than another ear disorder.

(Option 1). Redness and swelling of the tympanic membrane are more suggestive of middle ear inflammation or infection, such as otitis media, rather than cerumen impaction. Cerumen is located in the external auditory canal, not on the tympanic membrane itself.

(Option 2). An external auditory canal that is longer than normal is not a typical assessment finding for cerumen impaction. Canal length is an anatomic characteristic and does not indicate wax buildup.

(Option 3). The presence of edema in the external auditory canal suggests irritation, trauma, or infection such as otitis externa. Although edema may narrow the canal, it is not the defining feature of cerumen impaction.

Educational Objective
Cerumen impaction is identified by yellowish or brownish waxy material in the external auditory canal. Redness, swelling, and edema are more consistent with inflammatory or infectious ear conditions rather than impacted earwax.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Ear Assessment

answerChoiceList.choice: Redness and swelling of the tympanic membrane
An external auditory canal that is longer than normal
The presence of edema in the external auditory canal
A yellowish or brownish waxy material in the external auditory canal

questionList.questionText: A nurse is preparing to test the function of cranial nerve XI. Which of the following actions does the nurse take to test this nerve?
questionList.explanationText:
Cranial nerve XI, also known as the spinal accessory nerve, is primarily responsible for motor function of the sternocleidomastoid and trapezius muscles. These muscles control movements such as head rotation and shoulder elevation. Assessment of this nerve focuses on evaluating muscle strength and symmetry by applying resistance during movement. Dysfunction of cranial nerve XI may present as weakness in shoulder shrugging or difficulty turning the head against resistance. It is important to differentiate this nerve from others: cranial nerve XII controls tongue movement, cranial nerves IX and X are involved in gag reflex and swallowing, and cranial nerve X also contributes to phonation. Proper testing ensures accurate neurological assessment and identification of motor deficits.

(Correct Option 4). Placing hands on the client's shoulders and asking the client to shrug the shoulders against resistance directly evaluates the function of the trapezius muscle, which is innervated by cranial nerve XI. This test effectively assesses motor strength and integrity of the spinal accessory nerve.

(Option 1). Asking the client to stick out the tongue assesses cranial nerve XII (hypoglossal nerve), which controls tongue movement. Tremors or deviation would indicate dysfunction of this nerve, not cranial nerve XI.

(Option 2). Touching the posterior pharyngeal wall to elicit the gag reflex evaluates cranial nerves IX (glossopharyngeal) and X (vagus). This test is unrelated to the function of cranial nerve XI.

(Option 3). Depressing the tongue and asking the client to say "ah" assesses cranial nerve X (vagus nerve), which controls palate elevation and phonation. This does not evaluate the spinal accessory nerve.

Educational Objective
Cranial nerve XI (spinal accessory nerve) is tested by assessing shoulder shrug and head movement against resistance. It controls the sternocleidomastoid and trapezius muscles, making motor strength evaluation essential for its assessment.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Cranial Nerve Assessment

answerChoiceList.choice: Asking the client to stick out his or her tongue and watching the client for tremors
Touching the posterior pharyngeal wall with a tongue blade and noting the gag reflex
Depressing the client's tongue with a tongue blade and noting pharyngeal function as the client says "ah"
Placing his or her hands on the client's shoulders and asking the client to shrug the shoulders against resistance from the nurse's hands

questionList.questionText: At a health screening clinic, a nurse is educating a young woman about breast self-examination (BSE). The nurse determines that the client demonstrates understanding when she states that:
questionList.explanationText:
Breast self-examination (BSE) is a method used by individuals to become familiar with the normal appearance and feel of their breasts in order to detect any changes early. Effective BSE includes both visual inspection and systematic palpation. Inspection is performed in front of a mirror to observe for changes in size, shape, skin texture, or nipple appearance. Palpation should be done using a consistent pattern to identify lumps or abnormalities and is best performed in positions that allow optimal breast tissue spread, such as in the shower (when skin is slippery) and while lying down (when breast tissue is evenly distributed). While BSE is useful, it is not the only screening tool; it complements clinical exams and mammography. Proper technique and regular monthly performance improve early detection awareness.

(Correct Option 4). Monthly BSE includes inspection before a mirror and palpation both in the shower and while lying down. This demonstrates correct understanding of the comprehensive technique, ensuring both visual and tactile assessment of the breast tissue for early identification of abnormalities.

(Option 1). Performing BSE every other month is incorrect because BSE should be done monthly to maintain consistency and improve recognition of normal versus abnormal findings.

(Option 2). Performing BSE on the day menstruation begins is incorrect because the breasts are often tender and swollen due to hormonal changes, making assessment less accurate. The ideal time is about one week after menstruation begins.

(Option 3). Although BSE is helpful, it is not the only method for early detection of breast cancer. Other screening methods such as clinical breast examinations and mammography are also essential.

Educational Objective
Effective breast self-examination includes both inspection and palpation performed monthly. Proper technique involves examining the breasts in front of a mirror and palpating in both standing (shower) and lying positions to enhance detection of abnormalities.
questionList.subject: Community Health Nursing
questionList.title: Breast Self-Examination

answerChoiceList.choice: BSE must be performed every other month
BSE is performed on the day menstruation begins
Monthly BSE is the only way to ensure early detection of breast cancer
Monthly BSE includes inspection before a mirror and palpation both in the shower and while lying down

questionList.questionText: A nurse conducting an eye examination notes that the client exhibits rapid involuntary oscillating movements of the eyeball when looking at the nurse. The nurse documents this finding as:
questionList.explanationText:
Nystagmus refers to rapid, involuntary, rhythmic oscillation of the eyes, which may occur horizontally, vertically, or rotationally. It is commonly associated with neurological disorders, vestibular dysfunction, or certain medications and substance use. This abnormal eye movement results from disruption in the vestibulo-ocular reflex, which normally stabilizes gaze during head movement. Identification of nystagmus is important during eye and neurological examinations, as it may indicate underlying conditions such as brainstem lesions, inner ear disorders, or cerebellar dysfunction. In contrast, other eye findings such as ptosis, scleral icterus, and exophthalmos involve structural or color changes rather than movement abnormalities.

(Correct Option 2). Nystagmus is the correct term used to describe rapid involuntary oscillating movements of the eyeball. The nurse accurately documents this finding as it reflects abnormal eye movement often associated with neurological or vestibular issues.

(Option 1). Ptosis refers to drooping of the upper eyelid, usually caused by weakness of the levator muscle or nerve dysfunction. It does not involve eye movement abnormalities.

(Option 3). Scleral icterus is a yellow discoloration of the sclera due to elevated bilirubin levels, commonly seen in liver disease. It is unrelated to eye movement.

(Option 4). Exophthalmos refers to abnormal protrusion of the eyeball, often associated with conditions like Graves' disease. It does not describe oscillating eye movements.

Educational Objective
Nystagmus is characterized by involuntary oscillating eye movements and is often associated with neurological or vestibular dysfunction. It is important to distinguish it from other eye abnormalities such as ptosis, scleral icterus, and exophthalmos.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Eye Assessment

questionList.questionText: A nurse is preparing to listen to the apical heart rate in the area of the mitral valve in an adult client. The nurse should place the stethoscope at the:
questionList.explanationText:
Cardiac auscultation is performed at specific anatomical landmarks that correspond to the locations where heart valve sounds are best heard, not directly over the valves themselves. The mitral valve area, also called the apical area, is located at the fifth left intercostal space at the midclavicular line in an adult. This location corresponds to the apex of the heart and is the preferred site for assessing the apical pulse. Correct identification of cardiac landmarks is essential for accurate assessment of heart sounds such as S1 and S2, detection of murmurs, and evaluation of rhythm and rate. Other landmarks correspond to the aortic, pulmonic, and tricuspid listening areas, so correct placement of the stethoscope is necessary for precise cardiac examination.

(Correct Option 4). The fifth left interspace at the midclavicular line is the correct location for auscultating the mitral valve area and obtaining the apical heart rate. This site overlies the apex of the heart, where the sound of the mitral valve is heard most clearly in adults.

(Option 1). The second left interspace corresponds to the pulmonic area, where sounds from the pulmonic valve are best heard. It is not the correct site for listening to the apical pulse or mitral valve area.

(Option 2). The second right interspace is the aortic area, used to assess sounds from the aortic valve. This location is not used for auscultating the mitral valve.

(Option 3). The left lower sternal border is associated with the tricuspid area. Although important in cardiac assessment, it is not the correct landmark for the apical heart rate.

Educational Objective
The mitral valve area is auscultated at the fifth left intercostal space at the midclavicular line. This is the correct location for assessing the apical pulse in an adult client.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Cardiac Assessment

answerChoiceList.choice: Fifth left interspace at the midclavicular line

questionList.questionText: A female client is seen in the clinic for a gynecological examination. The nurse begins collecting subjective data. Which of the following topics does the nurse ask the client about first?
questionList.explanationText:
Subjective data collection during a gynecological examination should begin with the least sensitive and most general information to help establish rapport and promote client comfort. The nurse typically starts with the menstrual history, because it provides essential baseline information about the client’s reproductive health, including the last menstrual period, cycle regularity, duration, flow, and associated symptoms. This information helps guide further questions about possible pregnancy, hormonal status, menopause, and gynecologic concerns. Once trust is established, the nurse can proceed to more sensitive areas such as sexual history, obstetrical history, and specific symptoms like vaginal discharge. Using an organized sequence improves communication, supports accurate assessment, and reduces client anxiety during the interview process.

(Correct Option 2). Menstrual history is the topic the nurse asks about first because it is a routine, nonthreatening starting point for the gynecologic interview. It provides important foundational information about reproductive function and helps the nurse plan additional assessment questions in a logical and respectful manner.

(Option 1). Sexual history is an important part of the gynecological assessment, but it is more personal and sensitive. It is usually asked after initial rapport has been established through less intrusive questions.

(Option 3). Obstetrical history is also relevant, especially for clients with prior pregnancies, but it is not usually the first topic discussed. Menstrual history generally comes first because it is broader and more immediately establishes the reproductive context.

(Option 4). Asking about the presence of vaginal drainage focuses on a specific symptom and is more appropriate later in the interview when exploring current problems or complaints. It is not the best initial question for beginning subjective data collection.

Educational Objective
In a gynecological interview, the nurse begins with the least sensitive and most general questions to build rapport. Menstrual history is usually obtained first because it provides essential baseline information about reproductive health and guides the remainder of the assessment.
questionList.subject: Maternal-Newborn Nursing
questionList.title: Gynecological Assessment

questionList.questionText: An adult client undergoes various diagnostic tests to determine the pumping ability of the heart. The nurse notes that the results of these tests indicate that the client's cardiac output is 5 L/min. The nurse concludes that:
questionList.explanationText:
Cardiac output (CO) is the volume of blood pumped by the left ventricle per minute. It is calculated as heart rate (HR) multiplied by stroke volume (SV). Normal resting CO in a healthy adult ranges from 4 to 8 L/min. This value reflects the heart's efficiency in delivering oxygenated blood to tissues. Factors that influence CO include preload, afterload, contractility, and heart rate. Maintaining CO within this range is essential for adequate tissue perfusion and cellular metabolism.

(Correct Option C) The client's measured CO of 5 L/min falls within the established normal range (4–8 L/min) for an adult at rest. Therefore, the nurse correctly concludes that the client has a normal cardiac output, indicating adequate cardiac function and perfusion at this time.

(Option A) A low cardiac output is defined as a CO below 4 L/min in an adult. Values below this threshold may indicate conditions such as heart failure, hypovolemia, or severe valvular disease. Since 5 L/min is within normal limits, labeling it as low would be incorrect.

(Option B) A high cardiac output exceeds 8 L/min in a resting adult. Elevated CO can occur in hyperdynamic states such as sepsis, hyperthyroidism, anemia, or arteriovenous fistulas. The value of 5 L/min does not meet the criteria for high output.

(Option D) A blood transfusion is indicated to increase oxygen-carrying capacity in anemia or to restore intravascular volume in acute blood loss. Cardiac output alone does not determine the need for transfusion; moreover, a normal CO of 5 L/min does not suggest any immediate indication for blood products.

Educational Objective Normal adult cardiac output ranges from 4 to 8 L/min. A value of 5 L/min is within this range, indicating normal cardiac function. Recognizing normal hemodynamic values is essential for accurate nursing assessment and intervention.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Hemodynamics

answerChoiceList.choice: The client has a low cardiac output
The client has a high cardiac output
The client has a normal cardiac output
The client will need a blood transfusion

questionList.questionText: A nurse is preparing to listen to the breath sounds of a client. The nurse should:
questionList.explanationText:
Auscultation of breath sounds is a fundamental component of the respiratory assessment. Proper technique ensures accurate detection of normal and adventitious sounds. The client should be positioned comfortably, typically sitting upright or supine, to allow full chest expansion. The diaphragm of the stethoscope is used for high-pitched breath sounds, and it should be held firmly against the chest wall to minimize external noise. The nurse should instruct the client to breathe slowly and deeply through the mouth to enhance airflow and sound transmission. Each location must be assessed for at least one full respiratory cycle (inspiration and expiration) to adequately evaluate both phases of breathing.

(Correct Option D) Listening for at least one full respiration (inspiration and expiration) at each location allows the nurse to fully assess the quality, intensity, and duration of breath sounds. This technique ensures that subtle abnormalities, such as a prolonged expiratory phase in obstructive lung disease or diminished inspiratory sounds, are not missed. Skipping or truncating the assessment can lead to incomplete data collection and inaccurate clinical conclusions.

(Option A) Lying prone is not a standard position for auscultation because it compresses the anterior chest and limits full thoracic expansion. Preferred positions include sitting upright, leaning slightly forward, or supine. Prone positioning also makes it difficult to access anterior and lateral lung fields systematically.

(Option B) Instructing the client to breathe through the nose reduces the intensity and clarity of breath sounds due to the turbulence created in the nasal passages and the filtering of sound. Mouth breathing produces a stronger, more audible airflow that facilitates accurate auscultation of both normal and adventitious sounds.

(Option C) The bell of the stethoscope is designed to detect low-pitched sounds such as vascular bruits or diastolic heart murmurs. For breath sounds, the diaphragm is the appropriate chest piece, and it should be held firmly against the skin to eliminate extraneous friction sounds. Holding the bell lightly would not effectively transmit respiratory sounds and may introduce artifact.

Educational Objective Correct auscultation of breath sounds requires the client to breathe slowly and deeply through the mouth while the nurse uses the diaphragm of the stethoscope held firmly against the chest. The nurse must listen for at least one full inspiration and expiration at each designated location to ensure a comprehensive assessment.
questionList.subject: Health Assessment
questionList.title: Lung Auscultation

answerChoiceList.choice: Ask the client to lie prone
Ask the client to breathe in and out through the nose
Hold the bell of the stethoscope lightly against the chest
Listen for at least one full respiration in each location on the chest

questionList.questionText: A nurse is using an otoscope to examine the ears of a client. Which of the following findings indicates to the nurse that the tympanic membrane is normal?
questionList.explanationText: Ask for explanation
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Ear Assessment

questionList.questionText: A Mexican-American client with epilepsy is being seen at the clinic for an initial examination. The nurse understands that the primary purpose of including cultural information in the health assessment is to:
questionList.explanationText:
In nursing practice, cultural assessment is an essential component of holistic care. It focuses on understanding a client’s beliefs, values, traditions, and perceptions of illness. Different cultures may have unique explanations for diseases, such as spiritual, environmental, or biological causes. Recognizing these perspectives helps nurses provide culturally competent care, improve communication, and increase adherence to treatment plans. It is not intended to diagnose or confirm medical conditions but rather to understand how the client interprets their illness and health behaviors.

(Correct Option 4). Determining what the client believes has caused the epilepsy is the primary purpose of including cultural information. This helps the nurse understand the client’s health beliefs and explanatory model of illness, which is crucial for providing culturally sensitive care and ensuring cooperation with treatment.

(Option 1). Confirming a medical diagnosis is the responsibility of the healthcare provider based on clinical findings, not cultural assessment. Cultural data supports care but does not establish diagnoses.

(Option 2). While cultural information may assist in forming nursing diagnoses, it is not the primary purpose. Nursing diagnoses are based on clinical data and patient responses rather than cultural beliefs alone.

(Option 3). Identifying hereditary traits relates more to genetic and family history assessment rather than cultural assessment. Cultural information focuses on beliefs and practices, not genetic inheritance.

Educational Objective : Cultural assessment helps nurses understand a client’s beliefs about illness causation and health practices, enabling delivery of culturally competent and individualized care.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Cultural Competence

answerChoiceList.choice: Identify any hereditary traits related to the epilepsy
Determine what the client believes has caused the epilepsy

questionList.questionText: A nurse performing a physical examination is assessing the client for costovertebral angle tenderness. When the nurse percusses the area, the client complains of sharp pain. The nurse interprets this finding as most indicative of:
questionList.explanationText:
The costovertebral angle (CVA) is located between the lower ribs and the vertebral column and overlies the kidneys. During assessment, the nurse performs percussion over the CVA to evaluate for tenderness. Normally, this area should not produce pain. CVA tenderness is a classic sign of renal pathology, particularly kidney inflammation or infection, such as pyelonephritis. When inflammation is present, percussion causes sharp or severe pain due to irritation of the kidney capsule and surrounding tissues. This finding is clinically significant and helps differentiate renal conditions from other abdominal or pelvic disorders.

(Correct Option 4). Kidney inflammation is correct because CVA tenderness is strongly associated with renal inflammation, especially in conditions like pyelonephritis. The presence of sharp pain on percussion directly indicates involvement of the kidneys and surrounding structures.

(Option 1). Liver enlargement does not cause pain at the costovertebral angle. The liver is located in the right upper quadrant of the abdomen and is assessed through abdominal palpation, not CVA percussion.

(Option 2). Ovarian infection involves the pelvic region and may cause lower abdominal pain, but it does not produce tenderness at the costovertebral angle.

(Option 3). Spleen enlargement occurs in the left upper quadrant of the abdomen and is not associated with CVA tenderness, as the spleen is not located near the costovertebral angle.

Educational Objective : Costovertebral angle tenderness is a key clinical sign of kidney inflammation or infection. Nurses use this assessment to identify potential renal disorders such as pyelonephritis.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Physical Assessment

questionList.questionText: A nurse is performing a voice test. To carry out this procedure correctly, the nurse asks the client to repeat words that are:
questionList.explanationText:
The voice (whisper) test is a quick screening method used to assess hearing acuity. During this test, the nurse stands behind or to the side of the client to prevent lip-reading and asks the client to occlude one ear while testing the other. The nurse then whispers a set of words or numbers from a distance of approximately 1 to 2 feet from the ear being tested. The client is asked to repeat the words. This method evaluates the ability to hear high-frequency sounds and helps identify hearing impairment. Speaking too loudly, too far away, or directly in front of the client invalidates the test results.

(Correct Option 2). Whispering words from the client's side at a distance of 1 to 2 feet is the correct technique. This ensures accurate assessment of hearing without visual cues and maintains standardized testing conditions.

(Option 1). Speaking in a soft tone from 5 feet in front of the client is incorrect because the client may rely on visual cues such as lip-reading, and the distance is too great for a standard whisper test.

(Option 3). Speaking in a normal tone from 10 feet away is not appropriate for a whisper test and does not accurately assess hearing acuity using standard screening methods.

(Option 4). Whispering from 20 feet away is excessive distance and not part of the standardized technique, making the results unreliable and invalid.

Educational Objective : The whisper test is performed by asking the client to repeat whispered words from 1 to 2 feet away from the ear to assess hearing acuity accurately.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Hearing Assessment

answerChoiceList.choice: Spoken in a soft tone of voice by the nurse about 5 feet in front of the client
Whispered by the nurse from the client's side at a distance of 1 to 2 feet from the ear being tested
Spoken by the nurse from the client's side in a normal tone of voice about 10 feet from the ear being tested
Whispered at a distance of 20 feet by the nurse while he or she is standing in front of the client

questionList.questionText: A nurse is palpating a client's sinus areas. Which of the following sensations does the nurse expect the client to indicate that he or she is feeling during palpation if the sinuses are normal?
questionList.explanationText:
The sinuses are air-filled cavities located within the skull that help with voice resonance, humidification of air, and reduction of skull weight. During a physical examination, the nurse palpates the frontal and maxillary sinuses to assess for tenderness. In a normal condition, the sinuses are non-tender, and palpation should only produce a sensation of mild pressure without pain. Pain, discomfort, or headache during palpation may indicate sinus inflammation (sinusitis), infection, or blockage of sinus drainage pathways.

(Correct Option 1). Firm pressure is the expected normal sensation. When sinuses are healthy, palpation may be felt as pressure but should not cause pain, indicating that there is no inflammation or infection present.

(Option 2). Pain behind the eyes is abnormal and may indicate frontal sinusitis or increased sinus pressure, suggesting inflammation rather than a normal finding.

(Option 3). Pain during palpation is not expected in normal sinuses and is a sign of sinus infection or inflammation, making this an abnormal response.

(Option 4). Pressure producing an acute headache is also abnormal and may indicate severe sinus congestion or infection, not a normal sinus assessment finding.

Educational Objective : Normal sinuses are non-tender and produce only a sensation of pressure without pain during palpation. Pain indicates possible sinusitis or infection.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Head and Neck Assessment

questionList.questionText: A nurse assessing a client's eyes notes that the pupils get larger when the client looks at an object in the distance and become smaller when the client looks at a nearby object. How does the nurse document this finding?
questionList.explanationText:
The process described is known as accommodation, which is a normal physiological response of the eyes. It involves coordinated changes in the lens curvature, pupil size, and convergence of the eyes to focus on objects at varying distances. When viewing distant objects, the pupils dilate and the lens flattens, while for near objects, the pupils constrict and the lens thickens to improve focus. This response is controlled by the autonomic nervous system and is an essential component of normal visual function. It is commonly assessed during a neurological and eye examination.

(Correct Option 4). Accommodation is the correct term for this finding because it describes the normal adjustment of the eye to focus on near and distant objects, including changes in pupil size and lens shape.

(Option 1). Myopia refers to nearsightedness, where distant objects appear blurry. It is a refractive error and does not describe the normal pupillary response to focusing.

(Option 2). Hyperopia refers to farsightedness, where near objects are difficult to see clearly. This is also a refractive error, not a normal physiological response like accommodation.

(Option 3). Photophobia is sensitivity to light and may cause discomfort in bright environments. It is unrelated to changes in pupil size during focusing.

Educational Objective : Accommodation is the normal process where the eyes adjust pupil size and lens shape to focus on objects at different distances.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Eye Assessment

questionList.questionText: A client is brought to the emergency department after a motor vehicle accident. The client is alert and cooperative but has sustained multiple fractures of the legs. How should the nurse proceed with data collection?
questionList.explanationText:
In emergency situations, the priority is to follow the primary survey and ABCs (Airway, Breathing, Circulation) while simultaneously gathering essential information. The approach used is called a focused assessment, which integrates rapid history-taking and physical examination while initiating life-saving interventions. Because time is critical, nurses must collect only relevant, immediate data while providing care, rather than completing a full health history. This ensures that life-threatening conditions are addressed promptly while still obtaining necessary information to guide treatment.

(Correct Option 2). Asking health history questions while performing the examination and initiating emergency measures is correct because it allows the nurse to prioritize urgent care while collecting essential information simultaneously. This reflects proper emergency nursing practice using a focused and efficient approach.

(Option 1). Collecting a full health history before performing the physical examination delays urgent care and is inappropriate in an emergency situation where immediate intervention is required.

(Option 3). Completing a comprehensive history, including social support and coping patterns, is not a priority during emergency care and can delay critical life-saving interventions.

(Option 4). While emergency measures are essential, not asking any health history questions at all is incorrect. The nurse should gather focused, relevant information concurrently to guide treatment decisions.

Educational Objective : In emergency care, nurses perform a focused assessment, collecting essential health information while simultaneously initiating life-saving interventions.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Emergency Assessment

answerChoiceList.choice: Collect health history information first, then perform the physical examination
Ask health history questions while performing the examination and initiating emergency measures
Collect all information requested on the history form, including social support, strengths, and coping patterns
Perform emergency measures and not ask any health history questions until the client's fractures have been treated in the operating room
